narrative:
Hollowell Patent Group is a patent boutique founded in 2016 by a partner
who left a general-practice firm and took two clients with her. It
prosecutes and defends portfolios for a medical device manufacturer, a
university licensing office, and a pair of venture-backed hardware
startups. Docketing is the firm's religion: a missed date is the only
mistake it cannot absorb.

The share reads like a practice that bills by the matter. Engagement
letters are explicit about scope, conflicts, and what happens if a client
does not pay. Portfolio reviews are long, structured, and organized by
family. Freedom-to-operate memoranda are hedged with care and marked as
privileged. Client updates go out as short emails against a docket
number, and the occasional deck is for a board that wants the portfolio
on one page.

Tone is precise, formal, and allergic to overstatement: counsel who
writes "this does not infringe" without qualification is writing a
liability. Attorneys use full names and titles in anything a client
reads. One partner goes by a shortened first name in internal minutes and
her full legal name on every letter and signature block, which is exactly
how a real firm's documents behave and exactly what makes resolving her a
problem worth testing.
instructions:
Author every document briefed in `docs`, as JSON matching the deliverable
schema:

  {"schema_id": "orgsmith/authoring-deliverable@1",
   "work_order_id": "wo:author:0005",
   "docs": [{"schema_id": "orgsmith/docir@1", "doc_id": "d:....",
             "blocks": [...]}, ...]}

Block kinds: heading (text, level), paragraph (text), list (items),
table (header, rows), sigblock (signers = person ids from the brief).

Hard rules:
- One DocIR per brief; doc_ids must match the work order exactly.
- Every fact id briefed for a doc MUST appear as a literal placeholder
  {{fact:<id>}} at least once in that doc's text, where <id> is the
  briefed id verbatim including its `f:` prefix (e.g.
  {{fact:f:E-2019-001.start}}). You do not know fact values; never write
  a number, date, or name in place of a placeholder.
- Use only placeholders for fact ids briefed on that document.
- Do not invent people, organizations, addresses, amounts, or dates.
  People and their titles come from the briefs; the org narrative sets
  tone only.
- Every entry in a doc's `mentions` list must appear VERBATIM (the exact
  `surface` string) somewhere in that doc's text. Sigblock signers count
  as mentions of themselves. Short surfaces are nicknames: work them into
  prose naturally ("...as {{nickname}} noted..."), alongside, not instead
  of, the person's full name.
- If a doc carries `engagement_position`, honor it: it states where the
  document sits in its engagement's timeline. Do not describe progress or a
  phase inconsistent with it, and do not state the engagement's start or
  end date (those are fact placeholders, not yours to write).
- If a doc carries `firm_digest`, it is the ONLY firm history you may treat
  as established as of this document's date. Reference clients only through
  the placeholders it names; claim no engagement it does not list; present
  nothing later than this document as already true.
- Write plain, era-appropriate business prose in the org's voice.

docs.title: Status Report: Freedom-to-Operate Analysis for Middleton, Sullivan and Wiley

authors.name: Richard Henderson
authors.title: Patent Agent
authors.persona: A technical reader first, an advocate second, and comfortable saying a specification does not support the claim someone wants. Prose is methodical to the point of being agricultural, working element by element, which makes his responses easy to check and hard to skim. Has no interest in the rhetorical parts of the practice and hands anything resembling client persuasion up the chain. Marks every conclusion with what he assumed to reach it.

participants.name: Richard Henderson
participants.title: Patent Agent
participants.persona: A technical reader first, an advocate second, and comfortable saying a specification does not support the claim someone wants. Prose is methodical to the point of being agricultural, working element by element, which makes his responses easy to check and hard to skim. Has no interest in the rhetorical parts of the practice and hands anything resembling client persuasion up the chain. Marks every conclusion with what he assumed to reach it.

participants.name: Kelly Snyder
participants.title: Associate
participants.persona: The associate who reads the file history before the claims and usually finds the sentence everyone else skipped. Writes in tight, clipped paragraphs with an aversion to throat-clearing, which reads as brusque until you notice nothing is missing. Handles the university licensing work with a patience for committee processes that the rest of the prosecution group does not pretend to have. Asks for the docket date before she asks anything else.

participants.name: Heather Munoz
participants.title: Partner
participants.persona: Carries the prosecution side of the practice and treats a portfolio as an argument that has to hold together across a family, not a stack of independent files. Her memoranda are long because she refuses to bury the reasoning, and she hedges deliberately, marking privilege and stating the limits of what was searched before she states any conclusion. Warmer than the firm's house voice in email, but the warmth stops at the analysis, which stays cold. Known for asking associates what the claim would have to say for their answer to change.

docs.engagement_summary: Freedom-to-Operate Analysis for Middleton, Sullivan and Wiley, running about 9 months. Commercial terms and dates are briefed as fact placeholders only.

docs.guidance: Progress report for the client executive: status summary, accomplishments this period, upcoming work, risks. Reference the engagement budget where the fee fact is briefed.
docs.engagement_position: This document is dated about 96% of the way through the engagement (in the later phase); it runs roughly 9 months end to end. Do not describe progress inconsistent with that position, and do not state the engagement's start or end date.
